summary: This notebook demonstrates an optimized inference pipeline for the ARC-AGI competition using the CompressARC method. Instead of solving puzzles sequentially, it implements a dynamic memory and time-aware scheduler to parallelize execution across multiple GPUs and CPUs. It calculates per-puzzle memory usage, estimates step duration, and allocates the remaining time budget to maximize the number of steps per puzzle. The pipeline outputs a formatted submission.json and includes a comprehensive visualization module to compare model predictions against ground truth, along with automated accuracy statistics. Readers should learn how to efficiently manage hardware resources and time constraints for large-scale grid-based reasoning tasks.
competition_flow: Loads ARC-AGI test puzzles from JSON, runs the CompressARC model in parallel with dynamic memory/time scheduling to generate predictions, and outputs a formatted submission.json with optional visualization and accuracy metrics.
data_reading: Reads puzzle inputs and metadata from ../input/arc-prize-2025/arc-agi_{split}_challenges.json using Python's json module.
Loads ground truth solutions from arc-agi_training_solutions.json and arc-agi_evaluation_solutions.json for visualization and accuracy calculation.
data_processing: No explicit preprocessing or cleaning in this notebook; relies on external imported modules (preprocessing.py, solve_task.py, etc.) for task handling.
The notebook itself focuses on scheduling, memory/time budgeting, and JSON serialization for submission.
ensembling: Generates two attempts per puzzle via parallel execution; no explicit ensembling or post-processing is applied.
insights: Dynamic memory and time-aware scheduling prevents GPU underutilization and fits within strict competition time budgets.
Parallelizing puzzle solving across multiple GPUs and CPUs significantly increases the number of steps per puzzle compared to sequential execution.
Automated visualization and accuracy calculation streamline the evaluation of grid-based reasoning model outputs.
critical_findings: Solving puzzles sequentially underutilizes available GPU hardware and easily exceeds the 12-hour time limit, necessitating parallel execution.
